# Valhalla tiles

## Three hierarchy levels

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Valhalla partitions the world into a fixed grid at three levels:

| Level | Tile size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 4° | highway / motorway |
| 1 | 1° | arterial |
| 2 | 0.25° | local streets |

Higher levels hold sparser, longer edges (interstates); lower levels hold denser local streets. A long route walks mostly level 0 with brief drops into level 2 at the endpoints.
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](figures/hierarchy-grid.png){.nostretch}

[Same nested structure as on the [Valhalla docs page](https://valhalla.github.io/valhalla/tiles/).]{style="font-size:0.7em;"}
:::
:::

## Inside one tile

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
Each tile is one `.gph` file inside the bundle, named by its grid coordinates:

```
<level>/<aaa>/<bbb>[/<ccc>].gph
```

Concatenating the digits gives the tile ID; the level's grid size recovers the SW corner. The file name alone encodes "L2 tile at (lat0, lon0)" — no lookup table needed.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/tile-zoom.png){.nostretch}

[A 0.25° L2 tile over central Mississauga. Every road segment, intersection, turn restriction, and access rule for those streets lives in this one file.]{style="font-size:0.7em;"}
:::
:::

## A trip touches many tiles

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/trip-footprint.png){.nostretch}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
The same trip's pings overlaid with the L0/L1/L2 tiles they actually fall inside. Even a short regional trip sweeps:

- a couple of L0 tiles (highway hops)
- a handful of L1 tiles (arterial)
- many L2 tiles (dense endpoints)

Every tile is a file Valhalla has to read and decode to match this one trip.
:::
:::
